title: Duplicate Income Detection — Same Income in Multiple Evidence Sources
description: Detect when the same income amount appears in multiple evidence sources and would be counted twice if both sources were summed. The most common instance: salary appearing in both Form 16 and the AIS salary entry. These must be reconciled (one replaces the other) not summed. This rule runs after Source Reconciliation (R-0007 through R-0009) and uses the reconciliation output to determine which income items are the same item from different sources versus genuinely distinct income items.

logic.if: classified_income contains two or more entries that share the same: income_head AND income_source_type AND approximate_amount (within 5%) AND assessment_year
logic.then: flag as potential_duplicate; apply resolution_hierarchy: (1) if one entry has a document source (Form 16, 26AS) and the other is user-declared → remove user-declared, retain document-sourced; (2) if both are document-sourced from different documents for the same underlying income (e.g. Form 16 salary + AIS salary) → retain higher-authority source, mark lower-authority as reconciled_duplicate; (3) if amounts differ by > 5% → not a duplicate, treat as conflict (R-0007/R-0008 handles); log resolution in conflict_log with type duplicate_income and resolution_method
logic.else: no duplicates detected; proceed with classified_income as-is

authority.section: Section 14 (Heads of Income) and Section 4 (Charge of Income Tax)
authority.proviso: Income is taxable once — double counting a single income item inflates tax liability and is an error in the return

notes: Duplicate income is not the same as duplicate TDS (R-0011). Duplicate income means the same gross income amount being taxed twice. Duplicate TDS means the same TDS credit being claimed twice. Both inflate the return in opposite directions: duplicate income inflates tax, duplicate TDS inflates refund. The most frequent real-world case: a freelancer submits both an AIS business receipt and a manually entered income figure — if the AIS entry was already extracted by the engine, the manual entry creates a double-count.
